exemplar.title: To what extent can nudge theory be used to correct externalities to influence consumers to make healthier food choices?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can nudge theory be used to correct externalities to influence consumers to make healthier food choices?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, focused and effectively connected to the investigation throughout.
The topic is communicated accurately with a consistent, appropriate purpose and focus.
An appropriate range of relevant sources and methods is employed, with clear evidence of informed selection.
weaknesses: The sampling strategy for the primary survey lacks justification and broader representativeness.
Survey question design and scale selection are described but not explicitly linked to research objectives.
There remains slight ambiguity in the geographic scope beyond the single-school context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work demonstrates very strong focus and method overall: the question is sharp and sustained, sub-questions sharpen the enquiry, and the methodology draws sensibly on primary and secondary sources. Minor lapses in sampling justification and scope clarity prevent the award of the full 6, but the performance clearly sits in the top band, meriting a 5.

strengths: Secondary sources are mostly recent, reputable and well integrated to support the argument.
Economic concepts such as externalities and price elasticity are explained clearly and applied coherently.
Terminology (MSC, MPB, Pigouvian tax, bounded rationality) is used accurately and consistently.
weaknesses: One diagram mis-labels MSB/MPB, revealing an application error in graphical analysis.
Some assertions (e.g. of price elasticity) lack numeric calculation or formulaic justification.
Occasional uncited claims interrupt the otherwise mostly appropriate use of sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows clear and coherent knowledge of the topic and applies source material in a mostly relevant way. Two strands align with the mid-band (3â4) and one with the higher band (5â6), yielding an overall mid-high performance that justifies 4 marks out of 6.

strengths: A majority of research is appropriate and clearly relevant, with credible academic and institutional sources.
Analysis demonstrates coherent reasoning, linking diagrams, elasticity discussion and survey insights with logical conclusions.
The discussion offers reasoned policy arguments with partially critical evaluation (e.g. nudge fatigue, mixed tool recommendations).
weaknesses: Primary dataâs small sample size and lack of robustness checks limit research quality.
Depth of analysis is constrained by absence of statistical testing and uneven exploration of alternative explanations.
Some evaluative points (income-group behavioural differences) are asserted rather than rigorously tested.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of critical thinking fall within the 4â6 band. The work displays solid research relevance, clear analysis and partially critical evaluation, but is held back by methodological limitations. Positioned at the top of the 4â6 band, it merits 6 out of 12.

strengths: The structure follows EE conventions with title page, contents, labeled sections and bibliography.
Formatting is generally consistent: legible fonts, numbered pages, labeled figures and tables.
weaknesses: Minor structure flaws: duplicated figure captions, one missing section number and an unfinished sentence.
Citation style is inconsistent and the contents table has slight misalignment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation overall meets the expectations of the 3â4 band but exhibits several minor inconsistencies that prevent full marks. The performance is solid yet imperfect, justifying a score of 3 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is sharply formulated (âTo what extent can nudge theory be usedâ¦â) and tightly aligned with the discussion, appearing in the introduction and framing the analysis of primary and secondary data. Sub-questions (impact, income effects, sustainability) further sharpen the investigation. Although the wording is clear and manageable, slight ambiguity remains about geographical scope beyond the school survey, preventing the maximum mark; the questionâs operationalisation in methodology could be tighter. Hence, a strong but not perfect performance merits 5.

The essayâs topic is announced in the opening section with a concise statement of both the general theme (nudge theory for healthier eating) and the specific context (Malaysia). The purposeâassessing impact, demographic variation and sustainability of nudgesâis explicitly outlined and revisited in later sections, giving the work a consistent line of inquiry. Overall focus remains clear and appropriate for an Economics EE, meeting the top-band descriptor. Minor lapses in section numbering and an unfinished sentence in the conclusion do not materially hamper communication, so a score of 5 is warranted rather than the full 6.

Most secondary sources are recent academic papers or reputable institutional reports that clearly relate to externalities, elasticity and behavioural economics. The essay integrates these sources to corroborate primary findings (e.g., Chilean PED study supporting survey results). Nonetheless, occasional assertions lack citation and one diagram mis-labels MSB/MPB, signalling imperfect application. Overall relevance is âmostlyâ appropriate, sustaining a solid mid-high performance in the 3â4 band; 4 is awarded.

The report follows EE structural conventions: title page, abstract information, table of contents, numbered sections, introduction-methodology-discussion-conclusion sequence, figures, bibliography and appendices. Minor flawsâmissing page number for section 4, duplicated figure captions and an unfinished sentenceâindicate that although the structure is âclearly appropriate,â execution is not flawless, placing the work at the lower end of the 3â4 band with a score of 3.

Overall layout is consistent: legible font, line spacing, labelled figures and tables, page numbering and a reasonably formatted bibliography. Small inconsistenciesâmixed citation styles, slight misalignment in the contents table, and variable figure qualityâprevent full marks but the majority of formatting expectations are met. This aligns with the 3â4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

The essay presents a reasoned discussion of policy options (taxes, subsidies, nudges) and evaluates their relative merits, referencing equity and long-run sustainability. The conclusion acknowledges methodological limitations and suggests areas for further research. Nonetheless, evaluative depth is uneven; some key assumptions (e.g., behavioural responses across income groups) are asserted rather than critically tested, and the final paragraph ends abruptly. The work therefore demonstrates âpartially critical evaluationâ consistent with the upper end of the 4â6 band, meriting 5.

Analysis employs welfare diagrams, elasticity calculations and comparison of survey findings with secondary data, showing a logical chain of reasoning. However, depth is limited by the lack of statistical testing and by some over-generalised claims (e.g., long-term nudge sustainability). Alternative explanations are mentioned but not fully explored. Conclusions are mostly supported, yet at times rely on descriptive linkage rather than rigorous analysis. This corresponds to âclear analysis with mostly supported conclusions,â warranting a mid-band score of 5 within the 4â6 range.

The student demonstrates clear understanding of economic concepts such as externalities, price elasticity, merit/demerit goods and libertarian paternalism, explaining them coherently and applying them to Malaysian dietary issues. Occasional inaccuracies occur (e.g., MSB/MPB placement error), and some theoretical mechanismsâsuch as how specific nudges shift MPBâare asserted rather than fully analysed. The knowledge base is thus âclearâ and generally well supported, aligned with the 3â4 band at the upper end (score 4).

The bulk of the evidence comes from credible academic articles, institutional reports and a purpose-designed survey. These sources are generally pertinent to the research question and are woven into the argument. Nonetheless, the limited scope of the primary data and the absence of robustness checks constrain overall research quality. The work therefore fulfils the âmajority appropriate and clearly relevantâ requirement of the 7â9 band only partially; it sits securely at the top of the 4â6 band with a score of 6.

The student employs both primary (school-based survey) and secondary (peer-reviewed studies, institutional data) sources, demonstrating informed selection. However, the primary data set is small (n = 48) and limited to one school, and no statistical tests or reliability checks are undertaken. While the survey design is described, there is little justification of question choice or sampling method in relation to the research question. Consequently, the methodology is âgenerally relevantâ but lacks the rigour and breadth required for the top band, fitting securely in the 3â4 range at a score of 4.

Economic terminology (MSC, MPB, Pigouvian tax, bounded rationality, libertarian paternalism) is used accurately and consistently, underpinning the narrative. Definitions are usually precise; only a single truncated sentence on ânudgesâ interrupts clarity. Given this minor lapse, the strand still meets the 5â6 descriptor but does not quite reach the maximum; a score of 5 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The definition of nudges ends abruptly, indicating an incomplete sentence that undermines clarity; the student should finish and proofread this section.
Survey question choices and scale design are described without justification in relation to research objectives; the student should explain why these measures were selected.
The primary dataâs small sample size and single-setting context limit its relevance and robustness; consider expanding or triangulating with additional data sources.
Word count is provided as required, demonstrating adherence to the formatting criteria.
The explanation of PED >1 for soft drinks is accurate and demonstrates effective application of economic concepts to secondary data.
The discussion of positive externalities and how awareness shifts demand shows clear reasoning linking theory to survey insights.
Figure 1.3 misrepresents MSB as upward sloping; MSB should be parallel to MPB and shifted upward to reflect external benefits correctly.
Figure 1.4âs bar segments for neutrality (â3â) are not clearly distinguished from other colors; consider adding pattern fills or clearer labels.
The student clearly states the title of the investigation at the start, fulfilling the IB requirement for a titled report.
The integration of survey support for government intervention provides a coherent link between primary findings and policy discussion.
The table of contents omits the page number for â4. Secondary Researchâ, making navigation difficult and violating structural conventions.
The research question is articulated clearly and directly, providing a focused framework that guides the entire investigation.
The sampling strategy lacks justificationâthere is no explanation of how participants were selected or how representative the school survey is of the target population.
Figure 1.1âs legend and color coding lack sufficient annotation, making it hard to distinguish âmaybeâ responses; refine the legend for better readability.
The assertion of price elasticity is made without showing any PED calculation; include the formula $$PED=\%\Delta Q/\%\Delta P$$ and numeric values to substantiate the claim.
The student recognises ânudge fatigueâ and the need for evolving interventions, reflecting a critical evaluation of long-term sustainability.
The interpretation of mixed receptiveness to nudges captures nuances in consumer behavior and applies economic reasoning to externalities effectively.
Secondary research on global cost and externalities is well-integrated, demonstrating relevance and effective application of source material.
Figure 1.5 is clearly labeled and the welfare loss triangle is correctly identified, aiding the readerâs understanding of negative externalities.
The description of consumers ignoring externalities lacks a reference to behavioural models or evidence; incorporate bounded rationality theory or a citation here.
The theoretical contrast between nudges and Pigouvian taxes is lucid, connecting policy tools with economic efficiency concepts.
The discussion of adapting nudges to diverse preferences shows the studentâs ability to refine the research scope with demographic considerations.
The analysis of price and income effects on nudge efficacy demonstrates clear understanding of income elasticity in context.
Recommendation to employ a mix of policy tools shows evaluative depth and practical policy insight.
The linkage between ESG incentives and public health nudges illustrates innovative thinking in extending the researchâs applicability.
The final paragraph ends abruptly, truncating key future research directions; finish this thought to complete the conclusion.
The conclusion synthesises primary and secondary findings effectively, highlighting the complexity required for sustainable nudge design.
The acknowledgement of data limitations and potential biases provides an honest evaluation of methodological constraints.
Bibliographic entries exhibit inconsistent formatting (e.g., varying use of italics and spacing), which detracts from the overall presentation quality.
